terms.gloss: time prepositions — seit, vor, ab, bis, in, während
terms.why: Needed in every Lebenslauf line and every appointment you book. seit plus present tense is a standing exam favourite.

terms.gloss: prepositions that always take the accusative: für, ohne, gegen, um, durch
terms.why: Five words you never have to think about again — memorise them as a chant, not as a rule.

terms.gloss: consolidation week — no new material, only re-drilling and testing
terms.why: The week that stops small cracks from compounding. Score 80% and you move on; the rule is 80%, not perfection.

terms.gloss: two-way prepositions — accusative for movement (wohin?), dative for position (wo?)
terms.why: The difference between "ich gehe in die Küche" and "ich bin in der Küche". Automate it and a chunk of your case errors vanish.

terms.gloss: the German application letter — a tight genre with fixed moves and a polite register
terms.why: Write three, get them corrected, keep them. This is the deliverable the entire German track exists to produce.

terms.gloss: the Goethe-Institut’s official German certificate, A1 through C2
terms.why: The document employers and offices recognise without argument. B1 is the level your Ausbildung plan needs.

terms.gloss: adjective endings — the suffix an adjective takes when it stands in front of a noun
terms.why: The single biggest accuracy leak between A2 and B1. Ten drilled minutes a day beats staring at the table for an hour.

terms.gloss: indirect questions — a question wrapped in a polite frame, verb at the end
terms.why: The polite way to ask a stranger anything, and directly scored in the B1 speaking module.

terms.gloss: possessive articles — mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer
terms.why: You cannot talk about your own family, job or plans without them, and they share the ein-word ending grid.

terms.gloss: reflexive verbs — verbs that need mich/dich/sich: sich freuen, sich bewerben
terms.why: Feelings, plans and applications all live here. Without them you can state facts but not talk about yourself.

terms.gloss: the reflexive pronoun mich/dich/sich/uns/euch that certain verbs demand
terms.why: It also splits accusative from dative — ich wasche mich vs ich wasche mir die Hände. Worth ten minutes to get straight.

terms.gloss: separable verbs — the prefix detaches and flies to the end of the clause
terms.why: Your entire daily routine is one: aufstehen, anfangen, einkaufen. Nothing in English or Arabic prepares you, so drill rather than reason.

terms.gloss: nominalisation — turning verbs and clauses into nouns: beim Lesen, die Verbesserung
terms.why: The compression trick behind dense official German. Build them yourself and Behörden letters become readable.

terms.gloss: past perfect: hatte or war plus Partizip II — the past before the past
terms.why: Lets you order events in a story instead of listing them. It usually arrives holding hands with nachdem.

terms.gloss: the relative pronoun (der, den, dem, dessen) that opens a relative clause
terms.why: It looks like the definite article but takes its case from its own clause. That swap is the whole trick.

terms.gloss: to introduce yourself
terms.why: The opening of every Goethe speaking module and every conversation you will start in Germany.

terms.gloss: job advertisements
terms.why: Authentic B1 reading that doubles as market research: you learn what employers demand a year before you apply.

terms.gloss: error autopsy — going through your corrected mistakes one by one
terms.why: Cards built from your own errors are the highest-value cards in the deck. This is what makes everything else stick.

terms.gloss: consolidation — locking in what you already met instead of adding more
terms.why: Adding new material over an unstable base is how people reach B1 on paper and A2 in practice.

terms.gloss: a job advertisement
terms.why: Written almost entirely in passive and nominalised German, which is exactly why those two topics matter to you.

terms.gloss: dative pronouns — mir, dir, ihm, ihr, uns, euch, ihnen
terms.why: A tiny set with a huge payoff: asking for help, giving things to people, getting out of trouble.

terms.gloss: of your profession — genitive of dein Beruf
terms.why: The pivot point: from here your vocabulary work aims at a workplace, not at a certificate.

terms.gloss: communication — using the language in a real situation rather than on a worksheet
terms.why: The strand people neglect. Output from week four, however ugly, is what stops the other strands going stale.

terms.gloss: the würde/könnte/hätte/wäre forms — politeness and unreal situations
terms.why: Six forms buy you the register an Ausbildung email, an office phone call and the B1 Sprechen module all expect.

terms.gloss: a closed group of masculine nouns (Student, Kollege, Herr) that add -n or -en in every case but the nominative
terms.why: Small, finite and a favourite of exam correctors. Learn the list once and "mit einem Kollege" never costs you a mark again.

terms.gloss: ordinal numbers — der erste, der zweite, am dritten
terms.why: Every form, appointment and Lebenslauf line needs a date, and dates smuggle in free adjective-ending practice.

terms.gloss: prepositions — für, mit, auf, wegen; each one governs a fixed case
terms.why: Never store one without its case. Same discipline as learning an Arabic verb together with its harf jarr.

terms.gloss: irregular — forms that do not follow the standard pattern
terms.why: German has far fewer irregulars than it feels like, and they are the most frequent verbs, so SRS pays out fast.

terms.gloss: the past — as a time, not a tense name
terms.why: Talking about what you have already done is survival skill number one in an interview.

terms.gloss: skills — reading, listening, writing, speaking
terms.why: The exam tests four separately, so a weak one is a visible, fixable gap rather than a vague feeling.

terms.gloss: habits — the daily routines that turn study into acquisition
terms.why: Consistency beats intensity. The floor-mode day that keeps the streak is worth more than a heroic weekend.

terms.gloss: milestones — the fixed checkpoints everything funnels into
terms.why: Deadlines you can book. The exam calendar is the boss; everything else in the plan negotiates around it.

terms.gloss: a German coursebook series (A1–B1), used as written reinforcement here
terms.why: It is what Goethe Jakarta teaches from, so working through it aligns you with a possible future course.

terms.gloss: relative clauses — "der Beruf, den ich machen will"
terms.why: How you name exactly which person or thing you mean. Dense in B1 Lesen and hard to write a Bewerbung without.

terms.gloss: wo-compounds — worauf, womit, worüber: asking about a thing after a prepositional verb
terms.why: Without them you end up asking "auf was wartest du?", which is exactly the tell of a learner.

terms.gloss: word order
terms.why: German lets you move almost anything to the front — as long as the verb stays put. That freedom is only usable once the rule is automatic.

terms.gloss: automatic — done without conscious thought
terms.why: The real target for endings and word order. Knowing a rule and not having to think about it are different skills.

terms.gloss: verbs that take a dative object: helfen, gefallen, gehören, schmecken
terms.why: Store each as a whole phrase — "Wie gefällt dir …?" — and small talk stops stalling while you hunt for the subject.

terms.gloss: declension — changing articles, adjectives and nouns to show case, gender and number
terms.why: The machine German runs on. Nail it and reading stops being guesswork about who did what to whom.

terms.gloss: the technical language of a specific trade or field
terms.why: Two hundred words from your own field beat two thousand general ones in an interview, because they prove you can be trained.

terms.gloss: politeness — the register of Könnten Sie, würden Sie, Entschuldigung
terms.why: Six polite forms change how Germans hear you. It is the default register for offices, emails and exams.

terms.gloss: listening training
terms.why: Blind pass first, transcript second. The blind pass is the part people skip and the part that builds the skill.

terms.gloss: a conversation with an AI — KI = künstliche Intelligenz, artificial intelligence
terms.why: A partner who never gets bored, stays at your level and corrects gently. Perfect for the ugly-output stage.

terms.gloss: conjugation — changing the verb to fit its person: ich gehe, du gehst, er geht
terms.why: Arabic already trained you for person-marking, and German’s set is far smaller. This is a discount, not a hurdle.

terms.gloss: connectors — denn, deshalb, trotzdem, weil, and the different word order each demands
terms.why: What a marker hears as a structured argument rather than a shopping list of sentences.

terms.gloss: modal verbs — können, müssen, wollen, dürfen, sollen, mögen
terms.why: They triple what you can express and install the Satzklammer that Perfekt, Futur and the passive all reuse later.

terms.gloss: official practice exam papers — plural of Modellsatz
terms.why: Two full timed ones back to back is the dress rehearsal that turns exam day into a repeat performance.

terms.gloss: the past participle: gemacht, gegangen, gesprochen
terms.why: The word sitting at the end of every Perfekt and every Passiv sentence you will ever build.

terms.gloss: participles — plural of Partizip
terms.why: The top 25 irregular ones cover most of what you will ever say about yesterday. Pure brute-force SRS territory.

terms.gloss: preposition — a word like mit, für, auf that forces a case on what follows
terms.why: The case is arbitrary and must be memorised with the word. Guessing it is the most common B1 writing error.

terms.gloss: regular — following the standard -e/-st/-t/-en pattern
terms.why: Once the regular pattern is automatic, hundreds of verbs switch on at once with no extra work.

terms.gloss: the sentence bracket — conjugated verb in slot two, the rest parked at the very end
terms.why: Learn the shape once and Perfekt, modals, Futur and Passiv are the same frame with different filling.

terms.gloss: written, in writing
terms.why: The written channel is where you can be slow and precise — which is why journal work fixes errors speech cannot.

terms.gloss: daily routine — the shape of your day from waking to sleeping
terms.why: Narrating your own day aloud is the cheapest speaking practice there is, and it drills separable verbs for free.

terms.gloss: a German dual vocational training programme — paid work plus vocational school
terms.why: The whole point of this track. B1 is the language bar most Ausbildung places set before they will even read you.

terms.gloss: filled in — Partizip II of ausfüllen
terms.why: The word you meet on every German form you will ever be handed.

terms.gloss: pronunciation
terms.why: Whatever accent you build in week one is the one you store every future word in. Fix it before the vocabulary piles up.

terms.gloss: the der/die/das family — dieser, jeder, welcher — which all take the same endings
terms.why: Learn the family, not the members. One grid covers a dozen words you would otherwise memorise separately.

terms.gloss: discussion, debate
terms.why: Arguing a position in German is a skill separate from grammar, and it is explicitly on the B1 mark sheet.

terms.gloss: the ein family — ein, kein and the possessives mein/dein/sein — sharing one ending set
terms.why: Recognising the family is what turns three separate tables into one, and it is where the adjective ending rules split.

terms.gloss: the comparative — größer, besser, mehr
terms.why: Comparing is how opinions get expressed out loud. Cheap to learn, and it lifts you off the flat beginner list.

terms.gloss: the subjunctive mood — the "would/could" gear of German
terms.why: Politeness and hypotheticals from one small set of forms. Cheapest possible upgrade from sounding like a learner to sounding grown-up.

terms.gloss: the German CV — tabular, dated, no prose, photo optional
terms.why: The first sendable artefact of this whole plan. Draft it a year early and you get twelve months to improve something real.

terms.gloss: an official practice exam paper published free by the Goethe-Institut
terms.why: The only honest measure of readiness. Sit it under real timing — pacing is what fails people who know the grammar.

terms.gloss: the simple past — written narrative German, plus war/hatte/konnte in speech
terms.why: You read it far more than you say it. It is what stops B1 Lesen texts from having holes in them.

terms.gloss: set phrases and sentence openers you keep ready for speaking and writing
terms.why: They buy thinking time: you can start a sentence before you know how it ends, which is what fluency looks like under pressure.

terms.gloss: noun (also called Nomen) — always capitalised in German
terms.why: Each one hides a gender and a plural you must store with it. This is where German’s real difficulty is concentrated.

terms.gloss: the superlative — am größten, am besten
terms.why: Pairs with the Komparativ; together they are the grammar of preference, which every speaking module asks about.

terms.gloss: the verb system — conjugation plus the whole tense and mood machine built on it
terms.why: One lane of the tree runs from "ich bin" to "muss gemacht werden". Everything in it reuses the same bracket.

terms.gloss: to introduce; sich vorstellen = to introduce yourself (or to imagine)
terms.why: The reflexive version is the first thing you do in every exam, class and job interview.

terms.gloss: vocabulary — the stock of words you actually own
terms.why: Frequency first, then your field. Two hundred vocational words outperform two thousand random ones.

terms.gloss: the accusative case — marks the direct object, the thing being acted on
terms.why: Only the masculine article visibly changes (der→den). Your Arabic naṣb instinct already expects this; you just learn new forms.

terms.gloss: to set up
terms.why: One evening of setup that decides whether the next 450 hours compound or evaporate.

terms.gloss: a job application — usually a Lebenslauf plus a formal cover letter
terms.why: The thing B1 is for. Everything upstream in this tree is building the language to write one that gets read.

terms.gloss: Arabic: the preposition that governs a following noun’s case
terms.why: You already store Arabic verbs with their preposition. Do exactly that for warten auf and sich bewerben um.

terms.gloss: main clause — the conjugated verb is the second element, whatever comes first
terms.why: The iron law. Once it is reflex, simple sentences come out grammatical without you deciding anything.

terms.gloss: the command form: Geh! Gehen Sie! Geht!
terms.why: Directions, instructions and every polite Sie-request you will make at a counter or in a workshop.

terms.gloss: indirect
terms.why: Wrapping a question in a frame is the difference between polite and blunt in a German office.

terms.gloss: the base form of a verb: gehen, machen, sein
terms.why: The form that lands at the end of the clause after a modal, after werden and after zu. Expect to see it there constantly.

terms.gloss: compounds — words built by welding two or more words together
terms.why: German builds vocabulary by gluing. Once you can split a compound you can read words nobody ever taught you.

terms.gloss: subordinate clause — the conjugated verb goes right to the end
terms.why: weil, dass, wenn, ob and obwohl all trigger it. Nothing in Arabic or English prepared you, so start the daily drill early.

terms.gloss: Deutsche Welle’s free video course, A1 to B1 — the spine of this plan
terms.why: Free, complete and level-sequenced. There is no reason to pay for a course before you have finished it.

terms.gloss: the nominative case — the subject, the one doing the verb
terms.why: The form every dictionary gives you. Knowing when it is NOT nominative is the entire job of the other three cases.

terms.gloss: exams
terms.why: Goethe B1 has four modules that can be passed separately, which turns one big risk into four small ones.

terms.gloss: reflexive — the verb’s action points back at the subject
terms.why: German uses these where English does not, so they have to be learned as part of the verb.

terms.gloss: to write; writing — also the name of the written Goethe exam module
terms.why: Three fixed task types you can walk in already knowing the structure of. The most controllable marks in the whole exam.

terms.gloss: temporal, time-related
terms.why: Time expressions are compulsory in a Lebenslauf and in any account of what you have done.

terms.gloss: separable
terms.why: The prefix leaves the verb and waits at the end of the clause. Pure drill territory — no explanation will speed it up.

terms.gloss: adjective — a describing word; it takes an ending whenever it stands in front of a noun
terms.why: Those endings are the biggest single accuracy leak between A2 and B1. Every adjective before a noun is a decision you have to make.

terms.gloss: the alphabet — plus the German letter names used when spelling names aloud
terms.why: You will spell your name on the phone and at counters constantly. Practise it before you need it.

terms.gloss: greeting someone
terms.why: The first thirty seconds of any encounter, and the part people judge your German on before you have said anything real.

terms.gloss: endings — the suffixes on articles, adjectives and nouns that signal case, gender and number
terms.why: German hides its grammar in these two or three letters. Reading them fast is comprehension; writing them right is your B1 score.

terms.gloss: to please, to be liked — "Wie gefällt dir Berlin?"
terms.why: The thing is the subject and the person is dative. Learn it as a whole phrase and it never trips you again.

terms.gloss: a conversation
terms.why: Finishing one whole exchange without escaping into English is the week-4 milestone that stops you stalling in silent input.

terms.gloss: negation — saying that something is not the case, with nicht or kein
terms.why: Half of real conversation is what you do not want or cannot do. Two words, one rule, enormous coverage.

terms.gloss: participle — the ge-…-t or ge-…-en form used in Perfekt and Passiv, and as an adjective
terms.why: Always store it with its auxiliary, or you will end up saying "ich habe gegangen" for years.

terms.gloss: position — as in "Verb an Position 2", the slot a word occupies
terms.why: Counting slots is how you check your own sentences before they leave your mouth.

terms.gloss: pronouns — ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr, sie, Sie
terms.why: Nine words that every early sentence hangs off. The polite Sie among them is a social decision, not just grammar.

terms.gloss: to speak; speaking — also the name of the spoken Goethe exam module
terms.why: The one module you cannot cram. It rewards keeping the turn going far more than being flawless.

terms.gloss: nevertheless, all the same
terms.why: Takes position 1 and kicks the verb to slot 2. Getting that order right is what makes contrast sound deliberate.

terms.gloss: question words starting with W: wer, was, wo, woher, wie, wann
terms.why: Six words that turn you from a person being asked things into a person driving the conversation.

terms.gloss: article — der/die/das and ein/eine, the words carrying gender and case
terms.why: Every noun gets learned with its article or the case system has nothing to attach to. Wrong article = failed card.

terms.gloss: therefore, that is why
terms.why: Your everyday "so" — and it moves the verb, unlike English. One of three connector patterns worth drilling as a set.

terms.gloss: the genitive case — possession marked on the possessor: des Mannes, der Frau
terms.why: Arabic iḍāfa makes it intuitive. Speech dodges it with von; B1 reading and your Bewerbung will not let you.

terms.gloss: opinion — "meiner Meinung nach" = in my opinion
terms.why: The B1 speaking module scores whether you can hold a position for two minutes. That starts with this phrase.

terms.gloss: the spoken past: haben or sein in slot two plus the Partizip II at the very end
terms.why: How Germans actually talk about yesterday. Past tense is survival skill number one in any interview.

terms.gloss: the present tense
terms.why: It also does duty as the future in everyday German, so getting it automatic covers two tenses for the price of one.

terms.gloss: an exam
terms.why: A booked exam date is what converts a study plan into a deadline you actually respect.

terms.gloss: sentence structure — the rules for where each element is allowed to sit
terms.why: Word order is not decoration in German. Put the verb in the wrong slot and the sentence stops being German.

terms.gloss: strong (here genitive plural: "of strong verbs") — verbs that change their stem vowel
terms.why: ging, sprach, nahm, fand. Recognition matters far more than production: you read these constantly.

terms.gloss: to start
terms.why: A habit started at the right week costs nothing; started six months late it costs you the timeline.

terms.gloss: clock time — both official (14:30) and colloquial (halb drei)
terms.why: halb drei is 2:30, not 3:30. Getting that wrong costs you an appointment, not just a mark.

terms.gloss: during; while — takes the genitive as a preposition
terms.why: A genitive preposition is a register marker: it makes a B1 text read as B1 rather than dressed-up A2.

terms.gloss: of the profession — genitive of Beruf
terms.why: Genitive in the wild. You will see this ending on every job title in a formal document.

terms.gloss: letters — the written kind
terms.why: The Goethe A2 writing module is essentially this, so the fixed frame is close to guaranteed marks.

terms.gloss: to be allowed to
terms.why: The verb of workplace rules and permissions — you will hear it on the first day of any Ausbildung.

terms.gloss: ending — the case/gender suffix on an article, adjective or noun
terms.why: The smallest unit of German accuracy, and the one an examiner counts.

terms.gloss: first — neuter form of erste
terms.why: The first real conversation is a threshold, not a lesson. Everything before it is preparation.

terms.gloss: questions — also: to ask
terms.why: Questions are how you stay in a conversation you only half understand. They let you steer instead of being interviewed.

terms.gloss: the Goethe-Institut — Germany’s official cultural and language institute; it runs the standard exams
terms.why: Its Jakarta branch is where you will sit the real thing, and its free Modellsätze are your best practice material.

terms.gloss: to help — takes a dative object: "Kannst du mir helfen?"
terms.why: The single most useful sentence for surviving a situation you do not fully understand.

terms.gloss: unreal, hypothetical — as in Konjunktiv II irreal
terms.why: The grammar of weighing options: "wenn ich mehr Zeit hätte". The B1 speaking module asks for exactly this.

terms.gloss: could, was able to — Präteritum of können
terms.why: The modals stay in Präteritum even in speech, so this small set is spoken German, not just written.

terms.gloss: Arabic: the case a noun takes after a preposition
terms.why: Your reflex that a preposition changes what follows is exactly the German dative reflex. Same instinct, new forms.

terms.gloss: although
terms.why: Concession is an interview muscle: "obwohl ich noch lerne, kann ich schon…". Verb goes to the end.

terms.gloss: the passive: werden plus Partizip II — "das Formular wird ausgefüllt"
terms.why: The register of forms, rules and job ads. You cannot read a Stellenanzeige or a contract without it.

terms.gloss: to be supposed to, should
terms.why: How instructions get relayed to you: "Sie sollen das Formular ausfüllen."

terms.gloss: the two dots on ä, ö and ü — a genuinely different vowel, not decoration
terms.why: schon vs schön, Mutter vs Mütter. Skip the distinction and your plural sounds like a singular to the person listening.

terms.gloss: verbs
terms.why: German verbs are regular far more often than Arabic ones. The difficulty is not the form, it is where the form has to sit.

terms.gloss: to become — and the auxiliary of both Futur and Passiv
terms.why: Learn it once, use it three ways. It is the highest-leverage single verb after sein and haben.

terms.gloss: numbers
terms.why: Prices, times, dates, house numbers, phone numbers. A large share of A1 listening answers are simply a number.

terms.gloss: profession, trade, occupation
terms.why: The word at the centre of every Ausbildung conversation you will have: "Welchen Beruf möchten Sie lernen?"

terms.gloss: so that — introduces a purpose clause with a different subject; also "with it/that"
terms.why: Purpose is what every interviewer asks about. One clean clause beats three broken ones.

terms.gloss: the dative case — the indirect object, and the case after in/an/auf/bei when nothing is moving
terms.why: The case behind every "where I am" and "who I gave it to". Map it to Arabic majrūr after prepositions to get started fast.

terms.gloss: date — the calendar date, not "data"
terms.why: Written and said in a fixed order you can rehearse once and reuse in every form you ever fill in.

terms.gloss: cases — plural of Fall; the four grammatical roles a noun can take
terms.why: The declension spine. Every ending you drill anywhere in this tree is a deposit into this one account.

terms.gloss: the future tense: werden plus an infinitive at the end of the clause
terms.why: The real prize is werden itself, which turns out to be the engine of the whole passive system at B1.

terms.gloss: grammatical gender — der (m), die (f), das (n)
terms.why: Arabic already made you accept arbitrary gender; German just adds a third. No philosophy needed, only the article-always habit.

terms.gloss: to have — and the auxiliary for most Perfekt forms
terms.why: Two jobs in one verb, which is why it is worth over-drilling early.

terms.gloss: had — Präteritum of haben
terms.why: Pairs with war for describing how things used to be, which is most of what an interview asks about your past.

terms.gloss: listening — also the name of the Goethe listening exam module
terms.why: The module people fail while their grammar is fine. Audio runs once and there is no rewind.

terms.gloss: Arabic: the possessive construction, "the X of Y"
terms.why: German Genitiv is the same idea with different machinery, so you skip the conceptual stage entirely.

terms.gloss: case — the four roles (Nominativ, Akkusativ, Dativ, Genitiv) a noun can play in a sentence
terms.why: German marks the role on the article, not on the noun ending. That is the one structural difference from Arabic iʻrab you have to rewire.

terms.gloss: to read; reading — also the name of the Goethe reading exam module
terms.why: Pacing, not vocabulary, is what fails people here — and the module can be passed and banked on its own.

terms.gloss: module — one of the four exam parts: Lesen, Hören, Schreiben, Sprechen
terms.why: At B1 each module is passed and banked separately, so a bad day costs you one retake, not the whole certificate.

terms.gloss: to like — and its polite form möchte, "would like"
terms.why: möchte is how you order, request and ask for anything without sounding demanding.

terms.gloss: not — negates verbs, adjectives and definite nouns
terms.why: One half of the negation rule. Sorting it from kein is a week of drills and then a lifetime of not thinking about it.

terms.gloss: noun — the other everyday word for Substantiv
terms.why: Article, noun and plural travel as one unit on one card. That single habit protects your accuracy for a year.

terms.gloss: despite — takes the genitive
terms.why: Concession in one word, and a marker of formal register in a Bewerbung.

terms.gloss: full, complete — plural/weak form of voll
terms.why: A full paper under real timing tests something a chapter exercise never does: your pacing.

terms.gloss: because of — takes the genitive in careful German
terms.why: Colloquial speech uses it with dative; your exam texts should not. It is a cheap register upgrade.

terms.gloss: become / will — first person of werden
terms.why: The auxiliary that builds both future and passive. Worth recognising instantly in any form.

terms.gloss: from where
terms.why: The second question anyone asks you in Germany, right after your name.

terms.gloss: all
terms.why: Relative pronouns in all four cases is the densest structure in B1 reading — and where every case you drilled pays out.

terms.gloss: so, therefore — a false friend; it does NOT mean English "also"
terms.why: A classic trap for English speakers. Using it wrongly is a small tell that you are translating.

terms.gloss: doctor
terms.why: A doctor visit stops being optional once you live there, and the vocabulary is directly Ausbildung-relevant.

terms.gloss: that — introduces a clause and sends the verb to the end
terms.why: Reporting what someone said or thinks. Half of any conversation about other people needs it.

terms.gloss: because, for — keeps normal word order, unlike weil
terms.why: The connector that lets you give a reason without the verb-final gymnastics. Useful when speaking under pressure.

terms.gloss: end
terms.why: The end of the clause is where German parks its most important verb. That single fact reshapes how you listen.

terms.gloss: no, not a — negates nouns that have an indefinite article or none at all
terms.why: One clean rule covers most negation you will ever need. Fifteen mixed items a day for a week settles it.

terms.gloss: after; to (a place); according to — takes the dative
terms.why: Three common meanings, one case. High frequency, so worth over-learning early.

terms.gloss: Arabic: the accusative case, marked with fatḥa
terms.why: You already expect a noun to change when it becomes an object. German just moves the marking onto the article.

terms.gloss: without — always takes the accusative
terms.why: One of the five fixed accusative prepositions. No thinking required once the chant is in.

terms.gloss: to be — also the Perfekt auxiliary for movement and change-of-state verbs; and "his" as a possessive
terms.why: The most irregular and most used verb in the language. Everything in your first month hangs off it.

terms.gloss: oneself — the reflexive pronoun in a verb’s dictionary form
terms.why: If the dictionary shows sich, the verb needs it. Store it as part of the verb, never separately.

terms.gloss: because — sends the conjugated verb right to the end of the clause
terms.why: Giving reasons is the whole game: why this Ausbildung, why Germany, why now. Expect weeks of clunkiness; push through.

terms.gloss: if, when(ever) — for repeated or future events; verb goes to the end
terms.why: The als/wenn split is a trap examiners set on purpose. Learn the two as a pair, never separately.

terms.gloss: two
terms.why: Two full timed papers back to back is the point where exam day stops holding surprises.

terms.gloss: short for Akkusativ — the direct-object case
terms.why: Drill sheets label columns this way; recognising it saves a second per item, and you will do thousands of items.

terms.gloss: when (single past event); than (in comparisons); as (a role)
terms.why: Three jobs in three letters. The als-versus-wenn distinction is a standing exam question.

terms.gloss: contraction of an + das
terms.why: German contracts preposition and article constantly — recognising them is half of listening comprehension.

terms.gloss: on, onto — accusative for movement, dative for position
terms.why: One of the nine two-way prepositions. The case you pick is the whole meaning.

terms.gloss: at, near, at the home/premises of — takes the dative
terms.why: beim Arzt, bei der Arbeit. The everyday location word that pulls dative every time.

terms.gloss: the da-prefix that replaces a thing after a preposition: darauf, damit, davon
terms.why: Impossible to fake, and its absence marks you instantly as someone translating out of English.

terms.gloss: the (neuter) — also "that" as a demonstrative or relative pronoun
terms.why: The third gender English does not have and Arabic does not either. Learn it welded to the noun.

terms.gloss: short for Dativ — the indirect-object case
terms.why: The third column on every interleaved case sheet you will fill in from week 9 onward.

terms.gloss: the (masculine nominative / feminine dative and genitive) — also a relative pronoun
terms.why: One form, several jobs. The context tells you which, and reading that context fast is a B1 skill.

terms.gloss: the (feminine and plural) — also a relative pronoun
terms.why: The most frequent word in German. Every noun you learn must arrive with its own der/die/das attached.

terms.gloss: a, an (masculine and neuter)
terms.why: Where the article stops showing gender, the adjective ending has to take over. That handover is the A2 grind.

terms.gloss: with — always takes the dative
terms.why: The most common dative trigger in the language, so it is your best drill anchor for the case.

terms.gloss: new
terms.why: Also the marketing word on half the textbooks you will meet.

terms.gloss: short for Nominativ — the subject case
terms.why: You will see it abbreviated on every drill sheet and case table you use.

terms.gloss: place, location
terms.why: Answering "wo?" is the first job the dative does for you, and the most frequent one.

terms.gloss: and
terms.why: Neutral joining, no word-order effect — unlike deshalb or weil, which both move the verb.

terms.gloss: was — Präteritum of sein
terms.why: Past states without the clumsy Perfekt detour. One of five forms that instantly make you sound less translated.

terms.gloss: what
terms.why: Also the informal relative pronoun after alles, etwas and nichts.

terms.gloss: who
terms.why: The question word that keeps a conversation moving when you missed the name.

terms.gloss: how; as, like
terms.why: Also does the "as … as" comparison, where English speakers keep reaching for als by mistake.

terms.gloss: at, on (a vertical surface), to — two-way preposition
terms.why: an der Wand versus an die Wand: the case decides whether anything moved.

terms.gloss: contraction of in + dem — "in the" (dative)
terms.why: You will hear the contraction far more often than the full form, so learn it as its own word.

terms.gloss: in, into — accusative for movement, dative for position
terms.why: The first two-way preposition you meet, and the model for the other eight.

terms.gloss: whether, if — introduces an indirect question, verb at the end
terms.why: "Können Sie mir sagen, ob…" is the politest way to ask a stranger for information.

terms.gloss: at (a time); around — takes the accusative; also part of um…zu
terms.why: um sieben Uhr, um die Ecke, and "um … zu machen". Three high-frequency jobs.

terms.gloss: where
terms.why: The question the dative answers. Pair them and both get easier.

terms.gloss: to; too — and the particle in front of an infinitive
terms.why: The zu-infinitive is the polite written register in one structure, and the base of um…zu.
